what_i_got_wrong: Said percentages should be computed in the direction of the dependent variable
correct_rule: Compute percentages in the direction of the INDEPENDENT variable, across the dependent variable. Ask: of the people in this group, what % chose each option?

what_i_got_wrong: Calculated df by adding (r-1) + (c-1) instead of multiplying
why_i_got_it_wrong: Mixed up the operation — instinct was to add
correct_rule: df = (r-1) x (c-1). Think: how many cells are free to vary once you know the margins?

what_i_got_wrong: Said point estimate is 'any value in between' the CI
why_i_got_it_wrong: Didn't know that the point estimate is a single specific number
correct_rule: Point estimate = centre of the CI. Calculate as (lower + upper) / 2.

what_i_got_wrong: Said margin of error is the difference between the two endpoints
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused width with margin of error
correct_rule: Margin of error = HALF the width of the CI. Calculate as (upper - lower) / 2.

what_i_got_wrong: Got Type I and Type II errors backwards
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused false positive and false negative descriptions
correct_rule: Type I = false positive = convicting an innocent person (reject H0 when true). Type II = false negative = letting a guilty person go free (fail to reject H0 when false).

what_i_got_wrong: Couldn't set up a proportion test from a word problem
why_i_got_it_wrong: Didn't know how to calculate p-hat or recognise 'at most' as one-tailed
correct_rule: p-hat = count/n. 'At most' or 'no more than' → H0: p <= value, H1: p > value (upper-tail).

what_i_got_wrong: Didn't know when to use z-test vs t-test
correct_rule: z-test when sigma (population SD) is KNOWN. t-test when sigma is UNKNOWN and you use s (sample SD). In practice almost always t.

what_i_got_wrong: Only partially explained the big-n problem — said narrower CI but missed the key point
why_i_got_it_wrong: Knew the mechanics but not the implication
correct_rule: Large n can make trivially small effects statistically significant. Always report effect size alongside p-value. Statistical significance != practical importance.

what_i_got_wrong: Compared Levene's test p-value to 0.01 instead of 0.05, and used awkward phrasing
why_i_got_it_wrong: Confused significance threshold and didn't know the clean interpretation pattern
correct_rule: Levene's test: compare to 0.05. p > 0.05 = do not reject = variances equal = assumption MET. p < 0.05 = reject = variances unequal = assumption VIOLATED. You WANT an insignificant result.

what_i_got_wrong: Said you can rank which factor matters more by comparing eta-squared when interaction is significant
why_i_got_it_wrong: Didn't apply the interaction rule — main effects can't be interpreted in isolation when interaction is significant
correct_rule: When interaction is significant, the effect of one factor depends on the level of the other. Don't rank main effects. Instead, describe the pattern per segment using the marginal means plot.
